text: The da Vinci robotic system is used for an ovarian cystectomy. The surgeon operates with a bipolar instrument on the left side and a scissors instrument on the right side, without the use of a uterine manipulator.
The surgeon uses a left-sided Maryland dissector to puncture the simple ovarian cyst after dissecting the outer ovarian cortex. The cyst is aspirated without complications.

The surgeon punctures the ovarian cyst, allowing it to collapse. The collapsed cyst is held easily with an atraumatic grasper on the right side, while bipolar Maryland forceps are used on the left side. The surgeon carefully separates the cyst from the ovarian cortex.
The surgeon enucleates the cyst, using bipolar coagulation to control minor oozing. The dissection proceeds carefully near the cystic wall to avoid damaging the surrounding ovarian tissue.
The surgeon completes the separation of the cyst from the ovary, placing the fully enucleated cyst over the anterior cul-de-sac.
The surgeon lifts the uterus to examine the posterior cul-de-sac, confirming the ovary and fallopian tube are intact. The infundibulopelvic ligament is also lifted for examination.

The surgeon uses suction irrigation to remove cystic fluid, avoiding direct manipulation of the appendix by lifting it gently from the base to the tip. The suction irrigation device is operated from the right side port to suck out fluid and irrigate the area.
The surgeon uses a claw forceps through the right eight millimeter port to remove the cyst, pulling it out from the surgical field.
